reviews.review_text: Very little stress in this game, Great graphics, realistic as you want it to be.
The game is very realistic like when your in California and they give you tickets non-stop because your 1 mile over the speed limit.

I recommend the game, but I have some complaints about the new Illinois DLC. As a man that has driven many roads in this abhorrent state I have complaints. I personally hate Illinois, but I live here and don't like the injustice this DLC has done to it. For a starter I have not hit a single pot hole, or construction zone. The state police are also way to timid. In real Illinois they will pull you over for even thinking about making an infraction. All in all I give 10/10 and 
 I give the DLC a 7/10 would have been higher if not for the above comments.
Great game makes me feel like a true american. ive been waiting a year now for the lot lizard DLC cant wait to get my hog cranked by Barb gobbless
Great game especially if you can afford at least a steering wheel and a shifter, it feels really inmersive.

Graphics are good, reflection and shadowing clips a bit but doesn't take away from the game play experience.  This game is an investment, there are a lot of DLCs to collect, and the more you get into it, the more you will look at driving enhancements, the steering wheel, peddles and what not.  Overall, very much worth it.

I really looked forward to all the new states being added to american truck simulator until Illinois. I have over 800 hours in this game and a full driving set up for it. One of my favorite things to do was driving the world of trucking events that came with each new state. Seems to me SCS is doing to much to fast and forgetting that a lot of us only buy add on states day one for the community event and the cool in cab items / paint jobs that paired with the state. I won't be buying any more DLC's day one. If this is the trajectory SCS is going to take with future states I'll stick to my map mods and user created content. Sad to see a game running this long end up leaving the community behind (supposedly to focus on something else in the game and we shouldn't expect any events for quite some time). I know I'm not the only one upset by the missing event and "paying honor" to the state. SCS might have just lost any future purchases from me if they can't be bothered to keep the community in mind. Just sad that Texas, Oklahoma, Nebraska, Kansas, Arkansas, and several others got WOT events with their release but now its too much of a hassle. Congrats on slapping your community in the face. I'm not recommending this game for the simple fact they are pivoting away from community involvement and events that several people I know have come to love ( and expect ) from this studio.
The GPS sucks. I got a $12000 fine 50 ft from the finish on my first job because the useless GPS told me to go into a small parking lot I couldn't get out of

It's a cool game, but sadly it's like a Paradox game where you have to buy hundreds of dollars of worth of paid download content to make it enjoyable without mods. Also you have to download mods to make it realistic. 3 states for a base game is boring..

I liked the game for a bit but the game gets boring and stale qiuck with out thw $350 of dlc and it ends up feeling kinda like a scam since you arent given much with out them especialy graphically. not my thing solid 6/10 ignoring the dlc issue

🚛 🇺🇸 American Truck Simulator – look, I’m gonna be totally honest here: I’m not even a simulator fan. I usually play games for action or a good story, so spending hours just staring at asphalt isn't exactly my usual vibe.

But there’s something weirdly chill about this game. When you just want to turn your brain off after a stressful day, putting on a random radio station and driving a massive 18-wheeler through the neon lights of Las Vegas or the deserts of Arizona actually hits the spot. It’s pure relaxation, even if you’re just delivering a load of virtual gravel.

That being used said, ETS2 (Euro Truck Simulator 2) is just way better.

Driving through Europe feels so much more interesting and diverse. In ATS, you’re stuck with those endless, straight American highways where everything starts to look the exact same after an hour. ETS2 has those tight European roundabouts, crazy narrow country roads, and way more map variety. Plus, the trucks in ETS2 just feel better to drive compared to those giant American long-nosed rigs that turn like a literal cruise ship.

It's a decent game to zone out to, but if you've played Euro Truck, it’s hard to fully get into this one.

🟢 Perfect game to put on a podcast and just zone out
🌵 Beautiful desert sunsets and neon truck stops
🚛 Huge American rigs that make you feel like a badass
❌ ETS2 is just miles ahead in terms of maps and roads

6/10 – "Accidentally blocked a three-lane highway trying to park. Back to Euro Truck I go."

I get that not all buildings can be accurately imported.

But I bought the game (With the Oregon DLC) for a taste of my home. 

The drive from Medford to Klamath was like 2 seconds long. 

Klamath falls was completely inaccurate. 

Like  I said, I know better than to expect a 100% recreation of the towns. 

But damn, was I let down.

Unbelievably great quality of graphics. The game features beautiful scenery along with cutscenes and specified photo spots. it is a treat to drive through these picturesque roads. Great selection of trucks & trailers. My PC runs at 15-20 FPS will high quality settings and the gameplay is manageable.

American Truck Simulator is a really fun game where you drive big trucks around America and deliver different loads. At first I thought it would be boring because all you do is drive, but it's actually a lot more enjoyable than it sounds.
The graphics are nice and the roads, cities, and countryside look realistic. I liked driving through different states and seeing all the scenery while listening to the in-game radio. The trucks also look really cool and there are lots of customisation options.
The gameplay is simple. You pick up cargo, drive it to another location, and earn money. Then you can buy better trucks and grow your trucking company. Sometimes the journeys can be quite long, but that makes it feel more realistic.
One thing I didn't like is that the game can get repetitive after a while because you're mostly doing the same thing over and over. Some of the map expansions also cost extra money, which can be annoying.
Overall, I think American Truck Simulator is a great game if you like driving games and want something relaxing to play. It isn't full of action, but it's fun to explore the roads and build your own trucking business.
very good and realistic trucking game would highly recommend

Great game, good workshop mods.

Love hitting the road with a tall boy or two... for four.... well you get the point....

I play it on GeForce NOW and it's quite good.
Graphics and accuracy are nice, and I like being able to use my controller.
It is fun and has lots of interesting elements!
Best simulator ever, super fun to create a server and invite people.

American Truck Simulator is proof that driving a truck across the country can be more relaxing than most vacations.

On paper, it sounds like the most boring game imaginable. You pick up cargo, drive for hours, deliver cargo, and repeat. Yet somehow, it becomes incredibly addictive. One minute you're planning to do a quick delivery, and the next thing you know it's 2 a.m. and you've spent your evening hauling construction equipment across three states.

The attention to detail is fantastic. The trucks look great, the roads are beautiful, and the scenery can be surprisingly relaxing. Whether you're cruising through deserts, forests, mountains, or busy cities, there's always something nice to look at. It's basically a road trip simulator without the gas prices, traffic tickets, or questionable fast-food stops.

The progression system keeps you hooked. You start as a lone driver taking whatever jobs you can get, then slowly build your own trucking company. Before long you're buying garages, hiring drivers, expanding your fleet, and checking financial reports like you're running a Fortune 500 logistics empire instead of playing a video game.

The truck customization is excellent. You can spend hours tweaking engines, paint jobs, lights, interiors, and accessories. Nothing says "I've become emotionally invested in this game" quite like spending twenty minutes deciding which chrome mirrors best represent your trucking career.

Of course, the AI traffic occasionally reminds you that common sense is optional. Cars will merge into your trailer, slam on their brakes for no apparent reason, and generally behave as though they obtained their driver's license from the clearance section of a supermarket. Somehow, that just makes the experience feel more authentic.

The game is also surprisingly educational. I now know more about highways, freight routes, truck transmissions, and cargo logistics than I ever expected to learn. I downloaded a game to relax and accidentally became qualified to hold opinions about trailer configurations.

10/10.

I bought American Truck Simulator expecting a casual driving game. Instead, I became the owner of a virtual trucking company and now get excited about fuel economy, delivery deadlines, and finding the perfect parking angle. My friends are out saving the world in action games while I'm celebrating the successful delivery of 40,000 pounds of frozen chicken.

I gotta say, I love this game. It's so simple with a tiny bit of a learning curve at first. Really relaxing and immersive, great overall design, runs smooth, lots of customization and upgrades Love it 10/10.

# American Truck Simulator Review — A Road Trip Disguised as a Sim

American Truck Simulator is one of those games that sounds boring until you actually understand what it is doing. On paper, it is a trucking game. You pick up cargo, drive across long stretches of road, follow traffic laws, park trailers, and deliver loads. That does not sound like the most exciting pitch.

Then you play it, settle into the cab, watch the highway stretch out in front of you, and realize the game is not really about rushing anywhere. It is about the drive.

The best thing about American Truck Simulator is the atmosphere. Cruising across highways, passing city skylines, rolling through desert roads, catching a sunset, or pulling into a gas station after a long haul gives the game a calm rhythm that most games do not have. It is relaxing without being empty. There is always something to manage, but rarely so much that it becomes stressful.

The driving itself is satisfying because it has weight. Trucks feel big, turns need planning, braking matters, and parking a trailer cleanly feels genuinely rewarding. It is not an arcade racer, and that is the point. The fun comes from treating the job seriously enough that every delivery feels like a small accomplishment.

American Truck Simulator also has a great progression loop. Starting with smaller jobs and slowly building toward owning trucks, expanding your garage, taking better contracts, and improving your business gives the game a steady sense of purpose. It is not flashy progression, but it works because it matches the pace of the game.

What surprised me most is how easy it is to get pulled in. A short delivery can turn into another one, then another one, and suddenly you have spent hours just driving, listening to music, watching the road, and chasing the next payout. It is comfort gaming in the best way.

The game is not for everyone. If you need constant action, combat, or fast rewards, American Truck Simulator may feel slow. There are long stretches where the main gameplay is simply staying in your lane, watching your speed, and making sure you do not damage the cargo. But for the right player, that slower pace is exactly what makes it work.

For stream or video content, American Truck Simulator has a different kind of value. It is not constant chaos, but it is perfect for chill streams, conversation, background storytelling, community hangouts, and relaxed late-night content. It gives the streamer room to talk while still having enough gameplay structure to keep things moving.

My verdict is Hidden Gem.

American Truck Simulator is a game that knows exactly what it wants to be. It is calm, focused, surprisingly immersive, and much more engaging than the premise might suggest. It turns a simple job into a satisfying road trip, and that is why it works.

If you want a relaxing game with steady progression, great atmosphere, and a surprisingly strong sense of place, American Truck Simulator is absolutely worth playing.

Final Verdict: Hidden Gem
Amazing game however, IDK why, on my macbook pro 2026, it is not running 100% smooth. It has a little bit of stutter, and I can not solve it at all. Does not depend on graphic settings at all.

After playing ETS2 for over 100 hours now trying ATS. In ATS, the freeways are very wide, straight, and the views are expansive, compared to ETS2, where there are only trees on the left and right. Therefore, every location looks more detailed. The trucks are very detailed just like the Euro ones but the American trucks look cool, unique and have a spacious cabin. 

I really enjoyed going through Route 99 and Interstate 5 because of the detail that SCS created. Basically, California in this game is quite accurate, and so is Arizona. Because this game is taken from Google Street View. SCS can make other really nice open world racing games, sim or action games etc like GTA, Forza, Need For Speed, Train Simulator because you guys are good at making maps. I also skipped the night delivery just because to enjoy the scenery.

There's also a lot that needs to be improved in this game and ETS2.
- This game and ETS2 have almost no effects whatsoever: no exhaust fumes, no smoke from tires when spinning, no skid marks on the asphalt when braking hard, and so on.
- No flying dust effect on a deserted highway.
- Nevada needs a rework. The roads outside the city look low-quality with low-res mountain textures on the right and left, the bushes and vegetation also look low-quality. The roads are also low-quality. There are also a few areas in Arizona.
- It would be better if the truck could get dirty and go to the car wash to clean it.
- There's no visual damage.
- There's very little traffic in city. I also don't see any trucks without trailers.

As a former dump truck driver myself who still his CDL, I found this game to be pretty close to reality.  If you're wanting to learn how to drive a big rig, it simulates the challenges very well.  I do have a couple criticisms that could be resolved if the developers were so inclined.  One, as a former Public Works employee who worked in the utilities, the traffic lights are not synched up well and it can be excruciatingly tiresome sitting at stop light after stop light.  Also, about 25% of the time I receive a red light violation for a perfectly allowable right turn on red after a coming to a complete stop at the stop bar.  And getting an infraction for an accident that's clearly not my fault is a little annoying. As a completionist, I would also recommend allowing the ability to have more that 353 drivers in your company stable.  Just for fun, I was trying to acquire all available garages and fill them up with trucks and drivers but I hit the maximum far short of my goal and now my time with this game it over.   But overall, I would recommend this game.
pretty good game, nice to choose a job that'll take a while and kinda just zone out and enjoy the scenery

Incredible game. Genuinely speechless at how much dedicated work and effort SCS have put into this trucking sim. You get to experience the classic American trucking vibe with the Kenworths and Peterbilts and the modern next gen tech of the Volvo, all while driving through the scenic Western and Central United States. The maps are all very detailed and well done. The slight issue is that to unlock the full experience, you need to buy around 20 DLCs which can get a bit costly but its totally worth it! once again, incredible game and a MASSIVE pat on the back to SCS. Thank you.

I like the game a lot, but I HATE the AI.

NONE of the AI drivers know how to drive. AI cars and trucks will RANDOMLY slow to a crawl or a stop ON THE HIGHWAYS and FREEWAYS, without ANY roadblock or ♥♥♥♥ in the way at all. They just randomly STOP. They also don't follow the driving rules, they don't respect the right of way and they don't know how to follow road lights either.

The amount of time they run red lights is actually crazy. Its also crazy that I GET FINED when someone DRIVES IN TO ME, I can be waiting for a red light to go green or be using my right of way (ie it's not me who's supposed to hold back here) and get rammed and I HAVE TO PAY for it? A little ridiculous, I can't lie.

But those issues aside, i like this game. The trucks are more cooperative here than in ets2. They at least don't slip and slide all over the road when you drive unless you go crazy over the speed limit. (unlike ets2 where you can be below the speedlimit and the truck STILL slips and slides all over)

If you’re looking for a game to completely unwind after a long day, American Truck Simulator is honestly one of the most relaxing games out there. There’s something incredibly peaceful about grabbing a cup of coffee, hitting the open road, and just cruising through the beautiful landscapes the US has to offer. From deserts and mountains to small towns and massive cities, every drive feels like a little road trip adventure.

The sound of the engine, the calm music, and the endless highways create such a chill atmosphere that it almost becomes therapeutic. It’s not about rushing or nonstop action — it’s about enjoying the journey, watching the sunset over the road, and taking in all the scenery along the way.

American Truck Simulator turns something simple into an experience that’s cozy, immersive, and surprisingly addictive. Perfect game to relax, clear your mind, and just enjoy the drive.

Good ol' relaxing and fun truck drivin'.

What an ABSOLUTELY FANTASTIC trucking game! All of the trucks in this game are just S-tier. Great handling, realistic driving, need I say more? Alright, I will. In the 115 hours I've played this game, i've done many a delivery. So relaxing and chill, I can't recommend this game enough if you're just looking to unwind after a long day. Just boot this game up and make a delivery run! Hats off to the devs for crafting such a fantastic trucking game. I have absolutely no complaints. The AI traffic can be a little erratic on the road with the constant lane changes hahaha, other than that though? This game is a SOLID 10/10 for me.

I came into American Truck Simulator thinking it would just be “drive truck from point A to point B.”

Instead, it somehow became one of the most relaxing and addictive games I’ve played.

There’s something weirdly satisfying about cruising down long highways at sunset, listening to music or podcasts, watching rain hit the windshield, and slowly delivering cargo across huge American landscapes. This game turns simple driving into a vibe.

The map design is genuinely beautiful. Every state feels different, and the attention to detail is impressive. From deserts and small towns to giant highways and mountain roads, the atmosphere makes long drives surprisingly immersive.

And honestly, this game is therapeutic.

After stressful multiplayer games full of screaming, grinding, and chaos, ATS feels like a reset button for the brain 😭

No pressure.
No sweaty competition.
Just you, your truck, the road, and a GPS that occasionally tries to destroy your life.

The progression system is also more addictive than expected. Starting as a small driver taking basic contracts and slowly building your own trucking company feels rewarding. Buying better trucks, upgrading garages, hiring drivers, and expanding your business gives the game long-term motivation without feeling overwhelming.

The customization is fantastic too:

Trucks
Interiors
Paint jobs
Lights
Engines
Accessories
Mods

You can make your truck look either professional… or like a moving Christmas tree powered by diesel 💀

And the modding community deserves massive respect. Mods add realism, graphics improvements, weather systems, traffic packs, real company logos, maps, and so much more. The game becomes even better once you start exploring Workshop content.

What surprised me most is how calming this game feels.

Late-night driving while synthwave, country music, or chill playlists play in the background creates a strangely peaceful experience that very few games can match 🌃🚛

Of course, the game isn’t perfect:

Some gameplay loops become repetitive
AI traffic can be questionable sometimes
DLCs can get expensive if you buy everything
Driving for hours may not appeal to everyone

But that’s also the beauty of ATS.

It’s not trying to be explosive or cinematic every second. It understands its audience perfectly.

This is the kind of game where you accidentally say:
“I’ll do one more delivery.”

And suddenly it’s 4 AM.

Verdict:
One of the most relaxing simulator games ever made. Surprisingly immersive, incredibly atmospheric, and dangerously good for people who enjoy peaceful late-night gaming.

Now excuse me while I spend 45 real minutes delivering frozen chicken across Nevada like it’s a national emergency.
awesome game to relax to, especially great doing convoys with friends

The game is much better once you figure out which mods you'd like to add. There seems to be good support for the community. Good graphics. I wish they would add a mode to let you manage a few drivers and operate as an owner. Otherwise I am enjoying the game thus far.

I recently started playing on PC after being gifted an old Dell Precision m4800 from my mom's current boyfriend. I bought this game last year as a birthday present to myself. I don't have much of a life outside of home anymore, had the worst luck finding work especially this last year. This gives me something to do when there's nothing I can do, and I love every moment of it. As someone who comes from a family of truckers, the attention to detail is about as good as it gets, the physics are pretty well spot on and can always be tweaked with mods, and it runs well on lower end systems if you know what you're doing. For instance, I run this game at medium graphics between 30 and 60 fps depending on environmental factors in game and still manage to get deeply immersed. In other words, there isn't much else to say. If your tism lies with trucks and heavy machinery in general or you just want a whole different challenge outside of racing, give this a shot. Even lower systems manage well enough with enough tinkering
This game is SUPER realistic and offers an immersive driving experience. There are many aspects to the game and sooo much to do and explore.

Guys, find 20 bucks and ask your parents to order on gamestop and get the game if u need a giftcard. THIS GAME IS SO RELAXING and it feels so realistic and it is, you got multiplayer and you can make your own lobby for free. it not addicting, its relaxing. when your in a public lobby and you gotta do something, you gotta stop somewhere. but when your doing solo/career then you can click esc or something to pause the game no problem. Trust me i been waiting for this game and i did what i put at the start.

I usually play racing games—games where I have to go over the speed limit and drive recklessly to achieve first place. So, a game like American Truck Simulator is a great change of pace for me. I go from being an aggressive driver to driving cautiously and obeying traffic rules. At first, I thought this game was boring, but the more I played it, the more fun I had. It's a game where I can put a video on in the background and just relax. There's no pressure and no worrying about losing, just a relaxing simulation game.

8/10
